one_line_takeaway: Feature-space Smoothing certifies a lower bound on clean-vs-adversarial cosine similarity for frozen vision encoders via Gaussian smoothing, and a plug-in Gaussian Smoothness Booster (denoiser + residual mapper) substantially raises that certified radius and downstream empirical robustness for MLLMs (e.g., LLaVA captioning accuracy under FOA attack rising from 1% to 65%) without retraining the backbone.
key_finding: GSB substantially raises the certified radius derived from FCSB: at noise level sigma=0.5 the average certified radius R (for FCSB >= 0.5) rises from 0.101->0.455 (CLIP), 0.134->0.514 (SigLIP 2), and 0.009->0.363 (EVA-CLIP) after adding GSB (Table 1). Ablation shows both submodules of GSB matter: on CLIP-L14 with sigma=0.5, adding purifier P and mapper M together raises average R from 0.34 (FS alone) to 0.82 and raises certified accuracy at epsilon=1.00 from 6.6% to 50.0% (Table 7). Prediction-wise, FS achieves certified ImageNet accuracy comparable to randomized smoothing despite RS being classification-specific (e.g., SigLIP 2: FS 64.0% vs RS 67.6% certified accuracy at epsilon=1.00; Table 3), while FS additionally supports retrieval and other cosine-similarity-based tasks that RS cannot certify. On MLLMs, under the strongest attack tested (FOA, epsilon=16/255), FS raises LLaVA-1.5-7B captioning accuracy from 1% (unprotected) to 65% and cuts attack success rate from 94% to 6%; on image classification FS raises LLaVA accuracy from 3.8% to 87.2% and cuts ASR from 81% to 0.2% under FOA (Table 8). FS also generalizes across defenses: applying GSB on top of adversarially-trained FARE/TeCoA encoders further improves their robustness without any additional fine-tuning (e.g., Smoothed FARE on LLaVA raises FOA accuracy from 19% to 43% in captioning, Table 6), whereas the adversarially-trained encoders alone degrade sharply as attacks strengthen (LLaVA+FARE captioning accuracy drops from 44% under M-Attack to 19% under FOA). At the higher epsilon=32/255 stress test, Smoothed FARE still holds 48.4% classification accuracy vs 10.4% for FARE alone under FOA (Table 9).
core_contribution: The paper proposes Feature-space Smoothing (FS), a certified-robustness defense that operates at the level of a frozen feature encoder rather than at the classifier output. FS defines a smoothed encoder as the Gaussian expectation of the base encoder's output and proves (Theorem 3.2) a certified lower bound -- the Feature Cosine Similarity Bound (FCSB) -- on the cosine similarity between clean and l2-bounded adversarial feature representations. This bound is derived from a new 'Gaussian robustness score' of the original encoder (shown to be 1-Lipschitz after a Phi^-1 transform, Lemma 3.1), and is further propagated to prediction-level certificates (top-1 retrieval, general cosine-similarity targets; Corollary 3.3, Corollary 4.1, Theorem 4.2). Because most pretrained encoders (e.g., CLIP, SigLIP 2, EVA-CLIP, and MLLM vision encoders) have weak intrinsic Gaussian robustness, the paper also introduces the Gaussian Smoothness Booster (GSB): a lightweight plug-in module consisting of (1) a ~10M-parameter U-Net denoiser P trained to reconstruct clean input from Gaussian-corrupted input plus a robustness loss, and (2) a residual 'smoothness mapper' M (multi-head attention + depthwise conv + MLP blocks, k=3) trained with a robustness loss plus statistical/identity regularizers to raise the Gaussian robustness score without touching the frozen backbone. Both are trained with Monte Carlo-sampled Gaussian noise (n0=8) in a two-stage, self-supervised procedure (Algorithm 1). The method requires no retraining or fine-tuning of the protected encoder/MLLM.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to the protected feature extractor (denoiser, mapper, and frozen encoder), constrained to an l2- or l_inf-norm ball around the clean input. For the encoder-level experiments the attacker directly optimizes to minimize cosine similarity between adversarial and clean features using PGD-l2 and an AutoAttack-style adaptive APGD-l2 attack. For the MLLM experiments the paper adopts three SOTA white-box attacks specifically designed against MLLMs -- AttackVLM, M-Attack, and FOA-Attack -- with perturbation budget epsilon_inf = 16/255 (and an additional epsilon=32/255 stress test with FOA). Attack goals are targeted: mislead image captioning toward an adversary-chosen target caption, force misclassification to a targeted class, or force incorrect VQA answers.
limitations: The paper's own framing states the theoretical certification is at the feature level (cosine similarity to a bound), not directly at the autoregressive MLLM output; end-to-end certification of MLLM predictions is described as 'computationally expensive and challenging' and left uncertified -- the MLLM results (Section 7) are explicitly empirical, not certified. Certification degrades sharply as noise/attack strength increases (e.g., certified PSR falls to near 0% for epsilon>=0.375 at sigma=0.25 in Table 1). Retrieval certification on COCO is markedly weaker than ImageNet classification certification because of tighter decision margins between semantically overlapping candidate captions (Table 5 vs Table 3), which the authors attribute to intrinsic task geometry rather than a defense weakness. Gaussian noise itself measurably degrades clean accuracy of unprotected MLLM encoders (e.g., vanilla LLaVA clean accuracy drops from 92.4% to 80.4% under Gaussian corruption before any attack, Table 8), indicating a utility cost that GSB must actively counteract. The final certified robustness is 'encoder-dependent' -- tied to the backbone's intrinsic Gaussian robustness score -- so gains from GSB vary noticeably across encoders (e.g., EVA-CLIP starts from a much lower intrinsic score than SigLIP 2).

datasets_used: ImageNet (train subset: 5,000 images from 50 classes; val subset: 2,500 images from same classes, for GSB training/certification)
ImageNet classification downstream evaluation (500 images from 10 classes, for MLLM experiments)
MS COCO 5K test split (zero-shot image-text retrieval)
NIPS 2017 Adversarial Attacks and Defenses Competition dataset (100 images, image captioning)
quantitative_results: Average certified radius R at sigma=0.5 with GSB: CLIP 0.101->0.455, SigLIP2 0.134->0.514, EVA-CLIP 0.009->0.363 (Table 1)
CLIP-L14 ablation (sigma=0.5): FS alone avg R=0.34, certified acc @eps=1.00 = 6.6%; FS+denoiser+mapper avg R=0.82, certified acc @eps=1.00 = 50.0% (Table 7)
ImageNet certified accuracy @eps=1.00, sigma=0.5: FS vs RS -- CLIP 50.0% vs 53.0%, SigLIP2 64.0% vs 67.6%, EVA-CLIP 57.3% vs 54.5% (Table 3)
LLaVA-1.5-7B captioning under FOA attack (eps=16/255): accuracy 1%->65%, ASR 94%->6% after FS (Table 6)
LLaVA-1.5-7B image classification under FOA attack: accuracy 3.8%->87.2%, ASR 81%->0.2% after FS (Table 8)
LLaVA-1.5-7B + FARE captioning accuracy drop from M-Attack to FOA: 44%->19% (unsmoothed) vs FS mitigating this drop when combined (Smoothed FARE) (Table 6)
FOA attack at eps=32/255: LLaVA classification accuracy FARE alone 10.4% vs Smoothed FARE 48.4% (Table 9)
Empirical PGD/APGD attacks: 90,000 total generated adversarial images (6 epsilon values x 2 attacks x 3 encoders on validation set)
baselines_compared: Randomized Smoothing (RS) -- certified prediction baseline for ImageNet classification (Cohen et al.-style, cited as [18])
FARE -- adversarially trained robust CLIP encoder for MLLMs
TeCoA -- adversarially trained robust CLIP encoder for MLLMs
